comment.body: Hey, if you sell a VIX call to the dealers it basically hits index hedging the same way as being sold a 5–10 delta put. That VIX call sale loads dealers with extra upside gamma, which mechanically soaks up any VIX spike. Like she laid out in yday’s post.. picture a short-VIX-fly gamma landscape centered on the 16.5 strike. Below that linedealers got VIX gamma, and VIX vega flips to the downside, all while SPX vanna slowly drifts into positive territory. From there, a vol-selling feedback loop kicks in, not just VIX vega...

comment.body:
The thing is that I'm not eager to trade everything like an obsession.
Trading can give you high satisfaction-feeling, and makes your brain working, creating the illusion that you do something with your life, but in fact you just sits at the computer, and sometimes unnecessary risking.
As I showed in the example:

"You have $800 on your account. You go trading and you lose $300
Next day you win again $500
Next day you lose $200, but you get a successful 'revenge trading' and win $400
You get overconfident and lose $700 on the next day. 
And finally on the last day you can gain $300.

Now listen...
 -300 + 500 - 200 + 400 - 700 + 300 = 0"

Trade only when you have a clear vision. And if you have enough growth and money, don't put it at risk. 
Wait until it clears up. 
Big private funds sometimes do only 3 days a week. No edge in forcing
